nativeRange.habitat: Small, clear, slow-moving streams, creeks, and tributaries with sandy or fine-gravel substrates; often found among leaf litter and submerged roots in shaded areas

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (critical — hard substrates damage barbels)
leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
low-light floating plants (amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to diffuse light

diet.notes: Micro-predator and detritivore in the wild, sifting substrate for small invertebrates, worms, and organic matter. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and small frozen/live foods: microworms, baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, Moina, and Grindal worms. Sinking wafers and bottom-targeted foods are essential — this species does not compete well at the water surface. Feed once to twice daily in small amounts.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, chili rasboras)
freshwater shrimp (neocaridina, caridina — compatible with adults)
avoidWith: predatory catfish (piranhas, large plecos prone to aggression)
fin-nippers that target slow-moving bottom fish
large fast-moving fish that compete aggressively for food at the bottom

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference image of Corydoras habrosus showing characteristic blotched/mottled flanks and stocky body form.
Wikimedia Commons image; verify exact license and attribution on the file page before use.

summary: A tiny, sociable bottom-dweller (~3.5 cm) from Venezuelan and Colombian streams, distinguished from its 'pygmy cory complex' relatives by true substrate-hugging behavior and irregular blotched flanks rather than a horizontal stripe. Best kept in groups of 6+ with fine sand and a densely planted, low-flow setup.
careNotes:
Corydoras habrosus is the true bottom-specialist of the 'pygmy cory complex' — three small Corydoras species routinely confused in the trade:

1. C. pygmaeus (Pygmy Cory) — mid-water shoaler with a clear horizontal dark stripe running from snout to tail; actively swims in the water column.
2. C. habrosus (Salt & Pepper Cory / this entry) — bottom-oriented; identified by an irregular blotched or mottled pattern on the flanks rather than a continuous stripe; rarely leaves the substrate.
3. C. hastatus (Tail Spot Cory; see fw-fish-130) — mid-water rester, perches on plant leaves; has a distinctive dark caudal-peduncle spot with a pale halo.

True bottom-orientation is the most reliable behavioral distinguishing feature of C. habrosus from the other two species. All three are frequently mixed in trade bags and may be sold interchangeably as 'pygmy cory' — inspect specimens carefully.

Taxonomic note: A 2024 revision by Dias & Britto proposes moving a large portion of the Corydoras genus (including habrosus) into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma. As of 2026 this revision has not yet been universally adopted by all authorities; both Corydoras habrosus and Hoplisoma habrosus may be encountered in literature and retail.

Husbandry: Fine sand substrate is non-negotiable — coarse gravel rapidly damages the sensitive barbels used for foraging, causing infections and behavioral suppression. A group of at least 6 (preferably 10+) is strongly recommended; lone individuals or very small groups show reduced activity and increased stress. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12); replicate the low-TDS, tannin-stained conditions of Amazonian/Orinoco tributaries with leaf litter and driftwood. Temperature at the lower end of the range (22–24°C) is associated with better long-term health and longevity. Flow should be gentle; strong currents stress the species. Avoid copper-based medications — all Corydoras and callichthyid catfishes are sensitive. Regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) are important to maintain water quality without large parameter swings.

[Merge consolidation 2026-06-10]: Consolidated with retired entry fw-fish-487 (Salt-and-Pepper Pygmy Corydoras / Hoplisoma habrosum). Reclassified Corydoras habrosus → Hoplisoma habrosum by Dias et al. (2024); trade name "Habrosus Cory / Salt-and-Pepper Cory" retained.
breedingNotes: Breeding C. habrosus follows the typical Corydoras pattern but is less commonly achieved than for larger Corydoras species due to the tiny egg size. Conditioning a group with live and frozen foods (Daphnia, baby brine shrimp, microworms) for several weeks prior to a spawning attempt is recommended. Spawning is typically triggered by a water temperature drop of 2–4°C simulating the onset of the rainy season, achieved by performing a 25–30% water change with slightly cooler water. Eggs (1–2 mm diameter) are deposited singly or in small clusters on smooth surfaces including plant leaves, glass, and wood. Remove eggs or breeding adults post-spawning, as eggs and fry will be eaten. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days at 24°C; fry become free-swimming within a further 1–2 days and accept infusoria and very fine foods (vinegar eels, paramecium) initially, progressing to microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Fry are fragile and require pristine water conditions. Overall difficulty is moderate due to the small egg/fry size and sensitivity of early-stage juveniles.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras habrosus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Venezuela — upper Rio Meta, Rio Apure drainage; Colombia), adult size (~3.5 cm), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 2–12 dH, habitat description (clear, slow-moving streams with sandy/leaf-litter substrate), diet (small invertebrates, organic detritus), compatibility, and breeding behavior.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras habrosus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), adult size (male ~2.5 cm, female ~3.5 cm TL), native range (Venezuela, Colombia), and temperature range. Notes both Corydoras habrosus and the proposed Hoplisoma habrosus name per Dias & Britto (2024).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide from Aquarium Co-Op. Supplemented husbandry details on sand substrate requirements, group size recommendations (6+), copper sensitivity, and feeding approach for small corys. No dedicated C. habrosus page available.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras habrosus species profile
additional.notes: Species profile discussing the salt-and-pepper common name, distinguishing characteristics from C. pygmaeus and C. hastatus (the pygmy cory complex), natural habitat details, and aquarium setup recommendations including fine sand and leaf litter.

additional.name: Dias & Britto (2024) — Hoplisoma revision (via Catalog of Fishes)
additional.notes: Reference for the 2024 Dias & Britto taxonomic revision proposing transfer of a large Corydoras clade including C. habrosus to the resurrected genus Hoplisoma. Adoption of the new name is pending and not yet universal across trade and hobbyist literature as of 2026.

fish.breedingNotes: Conditioning with live/frozen foods followed by a 2–4°C cool water change to simulate rainy season onset; eggs (~1–2 mm) deposited on smooth surfaces; hatch in 3–5 days; fry require infusoria progressing to microworms/BBS nauplii. Moderate difficulty due to small fry size and water-quality sensitivity.
